title: Raman Study of the Influence of KCl and KI on Polarization Contours of OH Stretching Band of Water
abstract:
JOURNAL OF RAMAN SPECTROSCOPY, VOL. n, 
621-624 (1%) 
Raman Study of the Influence of KCl and KI 
on Polarization Contours of OH Stretching 
Band of Water 
Anna Sokolowska 
Laboratory of Molecular Interactions, Department of Chemistry, University of Warsaw, 1 ul. Pasteur, 02-093 Warsaw, Poland 
Changes in the isotropic and anisotropic contours of H,O stretching bands in solutions of KCI and KI were studied. 
The change of the isotropic contour of the OH stretching band in aqueous KCI solutions involves two parameters: 
the noncoiocidence parameter, Av and the Fermi resonance coupling constant, W. For KI solutions an additional 
change is caused by an increase in the distance between unperturbed vI and Zv, . Tbe noncoiocidence parameter Av 
decreases and changes its sign for the molar ratio of about 0.1 and the Fermi resonance constant W also decreases, 
when the electrolyte concentration increases. 
INTRODUCTION 
The contour of the OH symmetric stretching band of 
water depends on intermolecular resonance transfer and 
Fermi resonance. The non-coincidence effect in the 
vibrational spectra, that is, the wavenumber difference 
between the anisotropic and isotropic parts of the 
Raman spectrum, A v  = vanis0 - visa, is an interesting 
problem both experimentally and theoretically. Accord- 
ing to published theoretical treatment~l-~ 
the effect is 
due to the resonant transfer of vibrational excitation via 
the transition dipole moment, dp/dq, between the 
coupled molecules, when the conditions for resonance 
are fulfilled (the same wavenumbers and the same sym- 
metry type of vibrations). The other consequence is that 
the infrared wavenumber of a given vibration coincides 
with that of the anisotropic Raman component. Hence 
in a routine non-polarized Raman spectrum in which 
the isotropic part dominates, a non-coincidence 
between the infrared and non-polarized Raman bands 
appears. 
The symmetric stretching vibration of water has been 
found to be affected by intramolecular Fermi resonance 
and intermolecular resonant coupling.'-' 
Moreover, 
these two resonances interact with each other.'3.'4 
It has been noticed that electrolytes are able to 
change the sign of non-coincidence in many experimen- 
tally observed examples of electrolyte solutions in 
associated ~olvents.'~-'~ 
The change in the sign of the 
non-coincidence was explained by the 'third-body medi- 
ation' idea.'' In this paper, a study of the influence of 
anions on the non-coincidence for the symmetric 
stretching OH band of water is reported. 
EXPERIMENTAL 
The Raman spectra were recorded using a Cary 82 
spectrophotometer using 514.5 nm radiation from a 
CCC 0377-0486/96/080621-04 
0 1996 by John Wilcy & Sons, Ltd. 
Spectra-Physics argon ion laser for excitation. The 
power of the laser at the sample was 200 mW and the 
spectral slit width was 6 cm-'. A polarization analyser 
and a scrambler from the Cary 82 standard equipment 
were used for the polarization measurements. The 
amplification of the perpendicular component was four 
times higher than that of the parallel component. The 
isotropic and anisotropic parts were read point by point 
according to Iiso 
= Ill - 4/31, and Ieniso

corrected by the factor (yo - v ) ~ / v ,  where vo is the exci- 
tation wavenumber and v is the wavenumber shift from

the exciting line.Ig The 1 - exp( - hv/kT) factor was 
assumed to be equal to unity because all measurements 
were carried out at room temperature. 
The concentrations of electrolyte solutions were 
determined by pycnometric density measurements and 
by titration with 0.1 M AgNO, solution using K,CrO, 
as the indicator. The recorded contours were resolved 
into constituents using a modified Pitha and Jones 
program.,' The contours averaged from three records 
were processed with a computer. 
RESULTS A N D  DISCUSSION 
Figure 1 is a diagram of the Fermi resonance and inter- 
molecular resonance coupling interaction. The unper- 
turbed vu ('free OH bond') is split by intramolecular 
coupling of two OH bonds in water into a symmetric 
band v1 and an asymmetric v, stretching band. In turn 
v1 is split by intermolecular coupling into vls observed 
in the anisotropic part and vls observed in the isotropic 
part of the OH band. The intermolecular coupling splits 
the 2v, band also. In consequence, two Fermi reso- 
nances are obtained: the first between vla and 2v2, (and 
as a result v;, and 2v;, appear) and the second between 
vls and 2v2s (viS and 24,). Therefore, two components 
are observed in the isotropic part: v;, and 2v;,. Apart, 
the v;, and 2 4 ,  bands and also the v, band can be 
observed in the anisotropic part.

Laboratory of Molecular Interactions, Department of Chemistry, University of Warsaw, 1 ul. Pasteur, 02-093 Warsaw, Poland

The changes in the anisotropic part of the OH band are
smaller as the main contribution to this part has a v3
As model considerations show, the isotropic part of
the OH stretching band in aqueous solutions can be
resolved into two constituents and the anisotropic part
into three. However, a shoulder at high wavenumbers
can be observed (marked by an arrow in Fig. 2). This
shoulder was interpreted by %herera as a band of OH
with a weak hydrogen bond. Then the best fitting of the
OH contours needs to take into account the shoulder
and we should resolve the isotropic part of the OH
band into three and the anisotropic part into four con-
5 0 ~ , , , , , , , . , , , , , , , , , , , , . , , , , , , , , , , , . , , . . , , ,
Figure 7. Dependence of Fermi resonance coupling constant, W,
on molar ratio in solutions of (0) KCI and (0) KI in H,O. Line
and dots denote the changes in LiCl solution~.'~
The results of the resolution of the experimental con-
tours for aqueous KCl solutions are shown in Fig. 3
and for KI solutions in Fig. 4. To simplify Figs 3 and 4
the positions of high-wavenumber shoulders were
Using Eqns (1)-(4) we obtain the values of the wave-
numbers of vl, 2v2 unperturbed by Fermi and intermo-
lecular resonances, the non-coincidence Av = 2Y1 and
the Fermi resonance coupling constant W. In KCl solu-
tions the separation of v1 and 2v2 does not change with
molar ratio, as in LiCl  solution^'^ (Fig. 3), but in KI
solutions the separation of v1 and 2v2 increases when
the molar ratio increases (Fig. 4).
The changes of non-coincidence as a function of
molar ratio for KCI and KI solutions are shown in Fig.
5. The solid line denotes the changes of non-coincidence
in LiCl  solution^.'^ The non-coincidences in KCl and
KI solutions are the same as in LiCl.I7 The influence of
cations and anions on non-coincidence is the same. As
can be seen from Fig. 5, the non-coincidence becomes
negative for a molar ratio of about 0.1 and this effect
does not depend on the anion. None of the published
theoretical treatmentP anticipate the change of sign of
non-coincidence. Consideration of the repulsion forces
seem to be unjustifiable.2’*22 Kecki and Sokolowska
proposed the ‘third-body mediation’ idea” (Fig. 6)
based on a theory of Craig and Thir~namachandran.~
In direct contact of solvent molecules, the vibration
vector of the transition dipole moment induces an anti-
parallel vector in the coupled neighbouring molecule
and in this case the non-coincidence is positive. In indi-
rect contact when an ion as a ‘third body’ mediates in
the resonant transfer of excitation energy, the vector of
the transition dipole moment in the indirectly coupled
solvent molecule is parallel. In this case the non-
Figure 7 shows the changes in the Fermi resonance
coupling constant W as a function of molar ration. W
decreases as the molar ratio increases. As in Fig. 6, the
solid line denotes the effect of LiCl on W.I7 Neither a
cation” nor an anion effect was obtained for this
parameter W, just as for the non-coincidence, Av.
The influence of KCl and KI on the isotropic and
anisotropic contours of the OH stretching band of
water was studied. The isotropic contours were resolved
into three components and the anisotropic into four.
Based on the results of resolution the values of the non-
coincidence Av, the Fermi resonance coupling constant
W and wavenumbers of unperturbed v1 and 2v2 were
The non-coincidence decreases with increase in elec-
trolyte concentration. For a molar ratio of about 0.1 the
non-coincidence becomes negative. This effect is inde-
pendent of anion and cation species. The ‘third-body
mediation’ idea is proposed as explanation of the
change of non-coincidence sign.
The Fermi resonance constant decreases with the
electrolyte concentration and the changes do not
depend on the anion or cation.
The separation between unperturbed v1 and 2v2 is
independent of the concentration of electrolyte for KCl
but it increases with increase in concentration for KI.
The changes in the isotropic contour of the OH
stretching band of water in KCl solution are associated
with two parameters: non-coincidence and the Fermi
resonance coupling constant. For KI solutions the
changes in isotropic contour depend on three param-
eters : non-coincidence, the Fermi resonance constant
and the separation between unperturbed v1 and 2v2.
